data.title: Continued Backing Up Of U.S. Rates Extend The Greenback's Gains
data.description:
Overview

The persistent rise in US rates continues to help fuel dollar gains. The euro has been sold through $1.08 and the greenback has jumped over 1% against the yen to JPY152.75. It finished last week closer to JPY149.55. So far, Japanese officials have been fairly quiet, but this seems likely to change. The US two-year premium over Germany has widened by around 65 bps since late September to return to levels that prevailed in June. The greenback is firmer against all the G10 currencies, but the sterling is straddling unchanged levels. Emerging market currencies are weaker today.

The US 10-year yield (US10Y) is up a couple of basis points to 4.23%, while European benchmark yields are mostly 1-2 bps lower. The 10-year Gilt is an exception, and its yield is up almost four basis points to almost 4.20%. More dramatic moves are at the shorter end of the curve in Europe, where two-year rates are off mostly 4-6 bps today. The two-year Gilt yield is up two basis points to nearly 4.09%. There is more speculation about a 50 bp cut from the ECB in December, but the swap market is little changed from yesterday, reflecting a little less than a 45% chance. Meanwhile, Chinese (SHCOMP), Hong Kong (HSI), South Korea (KOSPI), and Australian (AS51) equities rallied in the Asia Pacific region. We note that the weakness in the yen is not sparking demand for Japanese equities as it appeared to earlier this year. Europe's Stoxx 600 (STOXX) is off for a third day, and US index futures are nursing small losses. Gold continues to rise alongside the dollar and US rates. It set a record high today near $2758.50. Lastly, after jumping around 4.5% over the past two sessions, December WTI has come back softer. It is slipping below $71 after poking above $72 a barrel yesterday.

Asia Pacific

"Feel mush, push," advised Lenin, "Feel steel retreat." And Beijing's wolf diplomacy may be taking it to heart. Australia's strategic alliance with the US while China is by far its largest trading partner is a contradiction and is the material of political novels and shows. After Australia called for an investigation of the origins of Covid, Beijing retaliated. Australia remained firm. China blinked and lifted the punitive tariffs it imposed in March. The levy on Australian wine was nearly 220%. Now that the tariffs have been lifted, exports of Australian wine to China have risen by a third in the first nine months of the year. India is the biggest buyer of oil from Russia. It is the second-largest supplier, after China, of restricted technology (microchips, circuits, and machine tools), yet China and India's relationship has been strained since the lethal border dispute in 2020. On the eve of the BRICS summit, China and India reached a deal to ease border tensions. This could set the groundwork for the first Xi and Modi formal bilateral meeting since 2022. Separately, official Chinese data show that mainland companies sold the most foreign exchange to banks since the end of 2021 last month. The depreciation of the yuan and the low interest rates have provided incentives for Chinese companies to retain their foreign currency earnings. However, the combination of the decline in US rates, the recovery of the yen, and the stimulus measures in China helped spur a short squeeze: Chinese companies to repatriate funds. Of course, media reports of Chinese banks buying foreign currencies from Chinese companies, which the US Treasury has cited in past reports, are often confused for intervention.

The continued rise in the US 10-year yield has helped send the dollar to JPY152.75, its highest level since the end of July. This is the third consecutive week the dollar is risen against the yen and the fifth week in the past six. The weaker yen is not lifting Japanese equities as it seemed to earlier this year. The benchmark three-month implied volatility has begun rising again. It is back above 11%. Official Japanese warnings may increase. The dollar is above the 200-day moving average (~JPY151.35). The next important chart area is JPY153.40-JPY155. Despite the broad strength of the dollar, the Australian dollar appeared to have forged a base near $0.6650 in recent days. However, it remains under threat today. A break could spur a quick move toward $0.6620-30, which holds the 200-day moving average and last month's low. For the past week, the US dollar has traded between roughly CNH7.1075 and CNH7.1475. It remains in that range today, and a break of that range may point the direction of the next five big figure move. The PBOC set the dollar's reference rate at CNY7.1245 (CNY7.1223 yesterday). Not counting October 8, when Chinese mainland markets reopened from the Golden Week holiday, on average, the fix has been 0.25% different than the previous day, which is on par with the pre-holiday fixings that showed an average change of a little less than 0.24%.

Europe

The news stream from Europe remains light. The euro and sterling continue to struggle to sustain even modest upticks. The double top is near $1.12 and the neckline is projected to $1.08. We initially thought that may prove to be too deep of a correction. Yet, the $1.08 area now appears to offer resistance, and the euro has been sold to $1.0780 today, and it does not look done. The market, pulling back on its aggressive pricing of Fed cuts and accepting that the sub-2% CPI gave the ECB more scope to cut rates, has spurred a dramatic adjustment of the currency-sensitive two-year interest differential. It took almost three months for the US premium to fall from 195 bps (mid-June) to 136 bps (mid-September). Yet, in the past five weeks or so, the premium has surged back to 195 bps. We anticipate that the surge is nearly over and expect a softer October employment report will help forge a top in the US premium. This may help the euro find support. The US two-year yield was a 40 bp discount to the UK in late September, as sterling reached a two-and-a-half-year high against the dollar (~$1.3435). It has approached parity and is now about a four basis point discount. Sterling has marginally surpassed the (61.8%) retracement of the leg up from the early August low (~$1.2665), which is found near $1.2965.

The next technical target may be the $1.0750 area. Options for 1.35 bln euros at $1.0755 expire tomorrow. The trendline drawn from the April and June lows is found today near $1.0765. For its part, sterling is trading a bit firmer than the euro, and there has been no follow-through selling today. Sterling recorded a low in North America morning yesterday near $1.2945. The trendline connecting the April and August lows comes in near $1.2910 today and $1.2935 at the end of the month. The (50%) retracement of the rally from the April low is slightly above $1.2865. A move above $1.30 would help stabilize the tone.

America

Given that Fed Chair Powell cited the Beige Book in his explanation, alongside CPI, in explaining why the central bank delivered a 50 bp rate cut last month, it may get a little more attention than usual. It may help cap the rising US rates to the extent it shows the disruptions from Hurricanes Helene and Milton. However, the larger event today is the Bank of Canada meeting. The swaps market is discounting about an 80% chance of a 50 bp cut today. It is tough to fade it, as the market has given the central bank an option. However, that option is not free and the prospects of a 50 bp cut (after 75 bp of cuts already this year) have weighed on the Canadian dollar. While the wage growth moderated, the September employment report was stronger than expected. Given the weakness of the Canadian dollar, and the fact that the US two-year premium over Canada is already the greatest since 1997, the latitude of Canada to run an independent monetary policy from the Fed looks limited. The Bank of Canada has surprised markets in the past, a 25 bp cut could see a jump in the Canadian dollar.

On a quarter-point cut, the US dollar could test the shelf forged last week near CAD1.3750. A break could target the CAD1.3650 area, and boost confidence that a high is in place. The swaps market is discounting a little more than a 1-in-3 chance of a 50 bp cut in December too. The forward guidance is important. A move above Monday's high (~CAD1.3850) leaves little in the way to deter a challenge on the high for the year set early August around CAD1.3945. Between last Friday and Monday, the greenback traded between about MXN19.65 and MXN20.0750. It traded well within that range yesterday. A weak retail sales report today, like the disappointing economic activity report (similar to a monthly GDP), could weigh on the peso. Tomorrow is the first half of October CPI. An uptick in the headline rate may play on fears of pass-through inflation from the weaker peso.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Kanye Anderson BDC: A Solid Newcomer To Add To Your Watchlist
data.description:
Introduction

When new companies IPO, I never pay too much attention to them now. While I keep an eye on them, I hardly give it much thought to look into their financials. I've been on the receiving end of major losses investing in newly IPO'd companies.

Case in point, I lost a lot of money investing in what I thought was the next hot thing years ago, Lucid (LCID). The stock is now trading under $3 a share. And despite selling out some time ago, the losses have somewhat scarred me. But looking to freshly IPO'd BDC Kanye Anderson (NYSE:KBDC), I must say I was impressed by what I saw so far.

As a BDC enthusiast, they're pretty high on my watchlist and I may look to add them to my retirement account in the future. In this article I take a look into the company's financials and fundamentals and discuss why dividend investors may consider adding this BDC to their watchlist as well.

Brief Overview

Kanye Anderson BDC is a newly IPO'd BDC, having gone public this past May. I know other BDC enthusiasts may not prefer this, but KBDC is externally managed which means they have to pay extra fees to an external manager. And while this is something I consider when looking to invest in BDCs, it's not a showstopper for me.

Externally managed BDCs can easily waive management and incentive fees, similarly to what Golub Capital BDC (GBDC) did, permanently reducing their incentive fee from 20% to 15%. This can give a BDC a cushion on forward earnings and make them more attractive for investors as well.

KBDC's external manager KA Credit Advisors is a subsidiary of Kanye Anderson and was founded 40 years ago in 1984. They are on the smaller side with $36 billion in assets under management, compared to other large asset managers like Ares Management (ARES) & Blackstone (BX), who have $447 billion and $1.1 trillion respectively.

Early Stages But So Far, So Good

Although the company has only been public a very short period, their fundamentals have been solid so far. During their second quarter earnings this past August, their net investment income missed analysts' estimates by a penny, coming in at $0.51. Total investment income also missed by $7.33 million, coming in at $52.5 million.

Year-over-year net investment income declined from $0.56. However, their total investment income grew solidly from $40,746 million over the same period. This was due to their growth in portfolio companies, which the BDC managed to grow from 73 to 106 at the end of the quarter.

Additionally, they made a significant amount of investment commitments compared to the prior year with $171 million in new commitments, compared to just $57 million in Q2'23. Sixty-seven million dollars of this was in new companies, up more than 50% from $31 million in the prior year's quarter.

Something else that impressed me was even though the company grew their portfolio company count, they managed to decrease their leverage to just 0.53x net debt to equity, well-below the sector median's 1.16x.

For comparison purposes, this is how they stacked up against some of their externally managed peers:

Oaktree Specialty Lending Corporation (OCSL) 1.10x

Ares Capital (ARCC) 1.06x

Blackstone Secured Lending 1.13x

KBDC managed to decrease their debt from $641 million to $622 million and had more liquidity available than total debt at $644 million. Forty-one million dollars of this was comprised of cash, strongly positioning the BDC for future growth ahead.

Furthermore, they have no debt due until 2027 with $425 million due in the months of February and June. So, from a balance sheet standpoint, Kanye Anderson BDC looks well-equipped to become a major player within the sector in the coming years.

Portfolio

Regarding their portfolio quality and borrowers, their companies were diversified, with each only accounting for 0.9%. Like several of its peers who Software and Healthcare Services make up the largest portions of their portfolios, Trading Companies & Distributors and Food Products make up Kanye Anderson's largest portions at 12.9% and nearly 10% respectively.

Their borrowers were much smaller in comparison to other larger, externally managed peers such as ARCC & BXSL. While both BDCs borrowers had weighted average EBITDAs of $165 million and $206 million respectively, KBDC's borrowers had a weighted-average EBITDA of just $58.9 million. But as a newer and smaller BDC, this is no surprise. And as they grow larger, I expect them to target larger borrowers.

This would not only likely position them to navigate economic downturns more successfully as larger borrowers are expected to see less financial distress and higher growth, it would likely drive price appreciation as investors gain more confidence in their operations.

So far, their smaller borrowers were holding up well in the higher for longer interest rate environment, with only 2 companies on non-accrual status. This rose from none in Q2'23 and this is something I will be keeping a close eye on moving forward.

Moreover, the company is likely to continue to grow by adding more borrowers, giving them higher chances of adding non-accruals. Additionally, their borrowers had a healthy 3.2x weighted-average interest coverage. One thing I did like about KBDC was their exposure to first-lien loans.

This stood at 98%, one of the highest in the sector. Below, you see how they compare to some more notable peers. Aside from Blackstone Secured Lending, Kanye Anderson BDC had the highest amongst their peers.

Company

First-Lien %

KBDC

98%

ARCC

50%

BXSL

98.6%

OCSL

84.3%

GBDC

93%

CSWC

88.7%

Dividend Coverage & NAV Growth

Kanye Anderson had ample dividend coverage of 127% during their latest quarter, despite the decline in NII from the previous year. And their management team seems confident in their ability going forward, declaring a special dividend of $0.10 this upcoming January and March 2025.

Their net asset value declined slightly from the previous quarter and year to $16.57, a discount to NAV of 3% at the time of writing. This stood at $16.63 in the previous quarter and $16.58 in Q2'23.

BDCs can often see volatility in their NAVs, especially over a shorter period of time. And considering the company suffered $0.11 dilution from their IPO, their NAV actually grew $0.05.

Moreover, for investors looking at their slight NAV decline as a negative, quality BDCs typically see solid NAV growth over time. This is a very important metric and if looking to invest in KBDC, this is something you should pay attention to as the BDC continues to grow.

Wall Street rates them a buy with a price target of $17 a share. But due to the upcoming lock-up period expiration, and lower interest rates, I think it's unlikely for Kanye Anderson BDC to see $17 in the near future.

Downside Risks

Although the BDC currently trades at a discount to its net asset value, KBDC is likely to see a further drop due to their pre-IPO shareholder lock-up expiration in Q2'25.

And because of this, along with their short track record, it's the reason I'm initiating a hold rating instead of a buy. Dividend investors are likely to receive a better entry point in the coming months. Combine this with lower interest rates, and you can see why they're a hold.

Conclusion

So far, Kanye Anderson appears to be a solid, fundamentally sound BDC. Their high exposure to first-lien loans, well-staggered debt maturities, and diversified portfolio with borrowers accounting for less than 1% allows them to likely weather volatility should the economy experience this going forward.

Additionally, their balance sheet enables them to be able to continue solid growth as they use their capital to make new investment commitments for the foreseeable future.

And despite their current discount to their net asset value price, I rate KBDC a hold due to lower interest rates and upcoming lock-up periods in the second quarter of 2025. As a result, I anticipate the BDC may see a lower share price, giving potential investors a better entry point in the months ahead.

data.title: Trick Or Treat: 3 High-Yielding REITs
data.description:
Everyone likes a high-yielding stock that has a stable business model and consistently pays dividends month-after-month or quarter-after-quarter.

Sometime these high-yielding stocks can be the delightful “treats” but occasionally, they can end up becoming nightmares.

Don't worry. Our job is to research these “high-yielders” to separate the wheat from the chaff.

Probably the most important metric to look at as it relates to dividend safety (and everything else) is to determine if underlying earnings is stable.

Can the firm continue to generate and grow its cash flow?

A conservative dividend payout ratio won’t do much good if the REIT is struggling to lease its properties and generate rent growth.

The next thing to look at is the dividend payout ratio by measuring what percentage of the company’s free cash flow, or adjusted funds from operations (“AFFO”), is being used to fund the dividend.

We always want to avoid these so-called “sucker yield” stocks.

Finally, we always look at the balance sheet to see if the company has too much debt.

One of the most significant metrics is the interest expense coverage ratio or the more comprehensive fixed charge coverage ratio, which includes preferred equity dividends and occasionally other miscellaneous expenses.

If the REIT is having a hard time servicing its debt obligations, then the dividend is probably in trouble.

Additionally, on the balance sheet, we like to see what the debt maturity schedule looks like.

What is the W.A. (weighted average) term to maturity?

Does the company need to refinance debt soon?

What is the company’s cost of debt? And so on.

A REIT with a tight payout ratio but a stable business model, including well-capitalized tenants and long lease terms, would be different from a REIT with a tight payout ratio and unstable tenants (see MPW), or a self-storage REIT that has short lease terms.

We like to look at REITs from multiple angles to access the quality of its earnings, its future growth trajectory, its balance sheet, and the safety of its dividend.

In this article, I wanted to look at a couple of high-yielding REITs that we're recommending.

Keep in mind, these are not SWAN (sleep well at night) stocks…

But they could be one day.

Armada Hoffler Properties, Inc. (AHH)

Armada Hoffler is a diversified REIT that specializes in the development and management of retail, multifamily, office, and mixed-use properties which are primarily located in Mid-Atlantic and Southeast markets.

The company is a major developer who builds mixed-use ecosystems where people can shop, work and live.

While AHH owns office properties, these are typically part of a mixed-use community surrounded by multifamily and retail properties. AHH strategically builds office properties that “fit” the surrounding ecosystem, which is intended to support office demand.

AHH’s stabilized portfolio comprises retail properties, office properties, and multifamily properties which are primarily located in Virginia, Maryland, and North Carolina.

On the right side of the chart below, the company breaks down its property count, net rentable SF, occupancy, and annual rent by property type.

The company’s stabilized portfolio includes:

  • 48 retail properties that cover 4.04 million net rentable SF and have an average occupancy of 95.4%
  • 14 office properties that total 2.3 million net rentable SF and have an average occupancy of 94.3%
  • 11 multifamily properties that contain 2,492 units and have an average occupancy of 94.9%.

Many of these properties are located in clusters to form mixed-use ecosystems. For example, the company owns retail, office, and multifamily properties that are all located in the Town Center of Virginia Beach.

At the end of the second quarter, the company had a weighted average portfolio occupancy of 94.9% and a weighted average lease term (“WALT”) of 6.6 years. The contractual rent payments owned to AHH have an average term of over 6 years, which will provide some stability in the company’s earnings.

The company has a stabilized portfolio debt to EBITDAre of 6.4x and a net debt to total adjusted EBITDAre of 7.5x.

Because AHH is such a large developer, some of its capital, including debt, is tied to projects under construction that are not presently generating cash.

I’m pretty sure this is why AHH breaks down its leverage ratio for both net debt and its stabilized, cash-flowing portfolio.

AHH has a net debt + preferred to adjusted EBITDAre of 8.4x and a fixed charge coverage ratio of 1.4x.

89% of the company’s debt is fixed or hedged, and it has a W.A. interest rate of 4.6% and a W.A. term to maturity of 3.6 years.

Until COVID-19 hit, AHH had a nice streak of annual dividend increases.

The company was forced to cut its dividend from $0.84 to $0.44 per share in 2020, but has raised it each year since. The 2Q24 annualized dividend comes to $0.82, almost back to its pre-pandemic level.

The company’s dividend payout ratio varies significantly depending on whether we use normalized FFO or AFFO as the benchmark.

According to FAST Graphs, 2024 AFFO is expected at $0.83 per share, just a penny over the 2024 dividend of $0.82 per share, giving us an AFFO dividend payout ratio of ~99%. This is very tight coverage and leaves very little room for error.

However, normalized FFO (“NFFO”) paints a much better picture. The midpoint of 2024 NFFO guidance of $1.24 gives us an expected dividend payout ratio of 66.13%.

AHH uses NFFO as its non-GAAP, bottom-line earnings number and provides full-year guidance in NFFO.

Typically, under these circumstances, I would use NFFO as a proxy for free-cash flow since normalized FFO is what the company reports. However, for dividend safety it's better to look at AFFO per share as it removes additional “real” cash charges such as tenant improvements, leasing commissions, and property related capital expenditures.

With an expected 2024 dividend of $0.82 per share:

  • Using FFO of $0.25 per share (annualized) gives us a dividend payout ratio of ~82%
  • Using Normalized FFO of $0.34 per share (annualized) gives us a dividend payout ratio of roughly 60%
  • Using AFFO of $0.21 per share (annualized) gives us a dividend payout ratio of approximately 98%.

There is no doubt that the AFFO dividend payout ratio is uncomfortably high, however AFFO per share is expected to increase by 1% in 2025 and then by 6% in 2026.

If these estimates are correct, by 2026 the AFFO dividend payout ratio will be improved to ~92%, assuming the dividend remains at $0.82 per share.

Armada Hoffler is not without risk, which is why the stock trades at a P/AFFO of 13.04x, translating into an AFFO yield of 7.67%.

Additionally, the company takes development risk and is relatively small with a market cap of $897.4 million. Plus, it has a hefty amount of debt, with a total enterprise value of $2.8 billion and a long-term debt to capital ratio of 71.89%.

On the other hand, the company develops high-quality mixed-use ecosystems in major Mid-Atlantic markets and has a solid dividend track record except for the cut during the pandemic.

This is definitely more on the high-risk / high-reward end of the spectrum. However, an investor willing to ride out the next several years may benefit from locking in a 7.28% dividend yield that will hopefully be sustained and should continue to grow over the long term.

In this case, I think the recent interest rate cut, and the expected future cuts will tip the scale in AHH’s favor, especially as it relates to its ongoing developments. There is a decent amount of risk, but given the earnings and dividend yield the market is currently offering, we think the risk is commensurate with the potential reward.

We rate Armada Hoffler Properties a Buy.

Broadstone Net Lease, Inc. (BNL)

BNL is a diversified net lease REIT that specializes in the acquisition and management of single-tenant commercial properties. The company invests in various property types, but places heavy emphasis on industrial properties.

In addition to industrial properties, the company invests in restaurants, healthcare, office, and retail. Broadstone was formed in 2007 and currently has a 38.5 million SF portfolio comprising 777 commercial properties that are 99.3% leased and located across 44 states and 4 Canadian Provinces.

BNL’s business has a lot of built-in stability, with 207 tenants operating in 53 industries with a weighted average lease term (“WALT”) of 10.4 years. Plus 94.8% of the company’s tenants provide financial reporting as an extra layer of protection, and its top 10 tenants only make up 20.2% of the total portfolio annualized base rent, or ABR.

Broadstone’s portfolio generates $385.5 million in rent per year. 54.6% of the company’s ABR is generated from industrial properties, 14.0% is generated from restaurants, 12.7% is generated from healthcare, 12.6% is generated from retail, and 6.1% is generated from office properties.

BNL has an investment grade balance sheet with a BBB credit rating from S&P Global. The company has strong debt metrics including a pro forma net debt to adjusted EBITDAre of 4.9x, a long-term debt to capital ratio of 39.06%, and a fixed charge coverage ratio of 4.7x.

BNL has minimal debt maturities through 2025 and a weighted average term to maturity of 4.1 years. Plus, at the end of the second quarter, BNL had $939.0 million of total liquidity.

BNL has increased its dividend each year since 2021 and has an average dividend growth rate of 4.79% over the last several years.

More importantly, BNL can easily cover its dividend with an expected 2024 AFFO payout ratio of 81.12%, which is slightly above its average AFFO payout ratio of 78.89%.

Broadstone has plenty of attractive qualities, such as its diversified portfolio of net lease properties, its focus on industrial assets, its investment-grade balance sheet, and minimal debt maturities over the next several years.

The net lease business model should afford plenty of stability, especially in light of BNL’s WALT of 10.4 years and high occupancy rate.

BNL trades discounted compared to most of its peers in the net lease space. The stock currently trades at a P/AFFO of 12.89x, compared to:

  • Realty Income (O): AFFO multiple: 15.33x
  • Agree Realty (ADC): AFFO multiple: 18.27x
  • NNN REIT (NNN): AFFO multiple: 14.45x
  • Getty Realty (GTY): AFFO multiple: 13.92x
  • Essential Properties Realty Trust (EPRT): AFFO multiple: 19.62x.

Additionally, BNL pays a higher dividend yield than many of its peers in the net lease space. Currently, BNL pays a 6.31% dividend yield that is secure with an expected 2024 AFFO payout ratio of 81.12%.

Analysts expect moderate growth over the coming years, with AFFO projected to increase by 3% in both 2025 and 2026. BNL has a stable business model, a strong balance sheet, a well-covered dividend, and a discounted price.

We rate Broadstone Net Lease a Buy.

CTO Realty Growth, Inc. (CTO)

CTO has gone through a significant transformation over the last century, and more so over the last several years.

The company’s history dates back to 1902 when it was formed near Dayton Beach, Florida. The company’s former name was Consolidated-Tomoka Land Co., and it primarily sold timber and mineral rights before it began selling off its land and buying income producing properties.

In May 2020, the company changed its name to CTO Realty Growth to better reflect its core business, which is to buy and lease commercial retail properties in fast-growing, business-friendly markets, with a particular focus on sunbelt markets.

Before its name change, the company made a strategic move to spin off its single tenant net lease properties to focus more on multi-tenant retail and mixed-use properties.

The net lease, single-tenant assets were spun off and used to form Alpine Income Property Trust (PINE) which is externally managed by CTO. This gave CTO a diversified income stream made up of rent checks and management fees. (see my recent article on PINE here)

At present, CTO is a small cap REIT engaged in owning and leasing retail properties, with a particular focus on open-air shopping centers located in business friendly, fast-growing markets in the Southeast and Southwest regions of the country.

CTO has a market cap of approximately $450 million and a 4.6 million SF portfolio comprising 22 retail properties that are 93% occupied and generate $92.3 million in ABR.

For a more detailed breakdown, CTO derives 94% of its ABR from multi-tenant properties, 96% from retail and mixed-use properties, and 31% from grocery-anchored shopping centers.

Additionally, the company generates 66% of its total portfolio ABR from the high-growth states of Georgia, Texas, and Florida.

At the end of the second quarter, CTO generated 62.5% of its rent from retail properties, 33.0% from mixed-use properties, and 4.5% from office properties. The company ended 2Q-24 with an occupancy rate of 92.6% and a WALT of 4.9 years.

CTO is a bit more leveraged than I prefer, with a net debt to pro forma EBITDA of 7.5x and a fixed charge coverage ratio of 2.7x.

As a general guideline, I prefer the leverage ratio somewhere between 5x and 7x and a coverage ratio above 3x. CTO is pretty close on both measures, so I’m not too concerned about the company’s debt at this time.

Additionally, the company has no floating rate debt and a reasonable cost of debt with an average interest rate of 4.23%. One of CTO’s strongest features is its debt maturity schedule, which is well-staggered, with no immediate debt maturities and manageable debt maturities over the coming years.

Since 2017 the company has had an average AFFO growth rate of 3.14%. Keep in mind that the company presently bears very little resemblance to what it looked like in 2017.

CTO struggled during the pandemic, as AFFO fell by -58% in 2020. The following year was also rough, with AFFO per share falling by -22% in 2021.

However, the company rebounded in 2022 with AFFO per share increasing 26%. Growth has since normalized with 4% AFFO per share growth in 2023 and 3% growth expected in 2024.

CTO trades at a multiple far removed from its peers in the retail sector. Currently, the stock trades at a P/AFFO of 9.98x, which pales in comparison to its competitors.

I think part of the issue is that CTO is a small company which warrants a discount, but mainly I think the issue is that CTO is a diversified REIT, which the market tends to discount.

While CTO owns office and mixed-use properties, it derives nearly 63% of its rent from retail properties and has properties located in very attractive sunbelt markets.

Shopping center supply has been lagging since the Great Recession, and the population in the sunbelt is expected to continue its trajectory of high growth.

A large population combined with low shopping center supply bodes well for the entire sector, but particularly multi-tenant retail properties where the population is booming, as it is in Georgia, Texas, and Florida.

CTO pays a 7.81% dividend yield that is very well covered by its cash flow. The company is expected to have an AFFO dividend payout ratio of 77.6% in 2024.

Put another way, at its current price the stock offers a AFFO yield of 10.02% and a dividend yield of 7.81%, so the company is generating more than enough cash flow to support its high yield.

We rate CTO Realty Growth a Buy.

In Closing

Never forget…

The safest dividend is the one that's just been raised.

As always, thank you for reading and commenting.

Happy SWAN Investing!

data.title: Wall Street Breakfast Podcast: McDonald's Shares Dip On E. Coli Outbreak
data.description:
Listen below or on the go on Apple Podcasts and Spotify

What investors need to know about the McDonald's (MCD) E. coli outbreak. (00:23) Spirit Airlines (SAVE) jumps on report of renewed merger talks with Frontier (ULCC). (02:17) Disney (DIS) reportedly not letting new users sign up via Apple's App Store. (03:18)

This is an abridged transcript of the podcast.

McDonald's (NYSE:MCD) disclosed on Tuesday that initial findings from an investigation into an E. coli outbreak indicate that a subset of illnesses may be linked to slivered onions used in the Quarter Pounder (AKA the Royale with Cheese) and sourced by a single supplier that serves three distribution centers.

The restaurant chain said that in line with its safety protocols, all local restaurants have been instructed to remove the product from their supply, and McDonald's (MCD) has paused the distribution of all slivered onions in the impacted area.

The Chicago-based company also temporarily removed the Quarter Pounder from restaurants in the impacted areas, including Colorado, Kansas, Utah and Wyoming, as well as portions of Idaho, Iowa, Missouri, Montana, Nebraska, Nevada, New Mexico, and Oklahoma.

The CDC said the outbreak involved 49 incidents across ten states, with one death and 10 hospitalizations being reported. The person who died after contracting E. coli was described by Colorado health officials as being "older" and with underlying conditions.

On Wall Street, TD Cowen analyst Andrew Charles said the firm's quick franchisee checks suggested McDonald's (MCD) is on top of the issue and that it seems largely contained. Charles said it is only natural to expect a short-term impact on sales.

Based on the food contamination analogies of Chipotle (CMG) in 2015 and Jack in the Box (JACK) in 1993, Charles and his team estimate that every 1% annualized change in U.S. same-store sales will lead to a $0.09 EPS impact. TD Cowen sees a worst-case scenario hit to Q4 EPS of $0.37.

Shares of McDonald's (MCD) are down more than 6% in premarket action.

Spirit Airlines (NYSE:SAVE) is up 17% premarket after a report that the discount airline has had early-stage merger talks with Frontier Group (NASDAQ:ULCC).

The two low cost carriers have had recent talks about a potential merger, according to a WSJ report on Tuesday, which cited people familiar with the matter.

If a deal between the carriers is agreed to, it would likely happen as part of Spirit (SAVE) restructuring its debt and other liabilities in a bankruptcy, according to the report.

The news comes after shares of Spirit (SAVE) skyrocketed on Monday after the carrier disclosed that it had landed a debt-refinancing extension with its credit card processor to extend a debt refinancing deadline to December 23.

Spirit (SAVE) attempted to merge with JetBlue (JBLU) only to have the merger shelved by a federal judge in January, who considered the marriage between two lower cost carriers as anti-competitive. Since that decision, Spirit (SAVE) shares have collapsed by 86%.

Frontier Airlines (ULCC) originally agreed to buy Spirit (SAVE) in February 2022 in a $2.9 billion cash and stock deal, though JetBlue (JBLU) ended up winning a nasty bidding war for the low-cost carrier.

Spirit Airlines (SAVE) has short interest of 33%.

Disney (NYSE:DIS) is no longer allowing new customers to sign up for Hulu or Disney+ via Apple's App Store.

Disney is now telling its would-be customers to pay for subscriptions on their own site instead of Apple's App Store. However, people who've already started paying for either service via Apple can keep doing that, the report said.

Apple takes up to 15% of the monthly fees from customers who sign up for the subscription services and Disney CEO Bob Iger back in May expressed he doesn't want to pay that anymore and wants to change the way his company looks at distribution.

More articles on Seeking Alpha:

Meta wins lawsuit dismissal over child safety allegations - report

Arm to cancel chip design license deal with Qualcomm over contract dispute - report

Oil industry averts Gulf of Mexico shutdown as judge extends environmental review deadline

Catalyst watch:

  • The healthcare sector will be in the spotlight, with the start of American Society of Nephrology Kidney Week and the AACR Symposium on Molecular Targets and Cancer Therapeutics.

  • Boston Scientific (BSX) will hold its Q3 earnings call at 8am and investors will see if the company gives any updates on the Axonics (AXNX) deal.

  • Tesla (TSLA) will hold its earnings conference call at 5:30pm. A point of major emphasis will be Elon Musk's commentary on the timing of a new mass-market cheaper electric vehicle, as well as the latest updates on FSD, energy storage business, and robotaxi developments. EV stocks with the tightest trading correlation to Tesla (TSLA) after earnings include Rivian Automotive (RIVN), Faraday Future Intelligent (FFIE), and NIO (NIO). Meanwhile, Uber (UBER) has had a negative trading correlation to Tesla (TSLA) on days with robotaxi developments.

Now let’s take a look at the markets as of 6 am. Ahead of the opening bell today, Dow, S&P and Nasdaq futures are in the red. Crude oil is down 1% at $71/barrel. Bitcoin is down 0.7% at $66,000.

In the world markets, the FTSE 100 is down 0.2% and the DAX is flat.

The biggest movers for the day premarket: Despite increasing its dividend by 7%, Starbucks (SBUX) shares dropped over 4% due to disappointing preliminary fourth-quarter results.
